results.title_inferred: Ume (梅): Japanese Plum in the Honzo Wamyo
results.original_text: 梅 — 和名 宇米 (Ume) — 味酸、平、無毒。主下気、除熱煩満、安心、止肢体痛、偏枯不仁、死肌、去青黒誌、蝕悪肉。
results.translation:
Ume [梅; romaji: ume] — Yamato vernacular name: 宇米 (ume). Flavor: sour [酸, san]. Nature: balanced/neutral [平, hei]. Toxicity: non-toxic [無毒, mudoku].

Therapeutic uses as recorded in the Honzo Wamyo: (1) Conducts and descends [counteracts rebellious] qi [下気, geki]; (2) eliminates heat, restlessness, and fullness in the chest [除熱煩満]; (3) calms and settles the heart-mind [安心, anshin]; (4) arrests pain in the limbs and body [止肢体痛]; (5) treats hemiplegia and numbness [偏枯不仁, henko-funin — literally 'withered on one side, without sensation']; (6) resolves dead or necrotic flesh [死肌, shiki]; (7) removes dark spots and blemishes on the skin [去青黒誌]; (8) erodes and dissolves malignant growths or putrid flesh [蝕悪肉, shoku-akuniku].

Note on Japanese usage: The Honzo Wamyo records ume as a plant widely cultivated in Japan by the early Heian period, introduced via continental channels (Tang-dynasty China and the Korean peninsula) but thoroughly naturalized and independently observed. Fukane no Sukehito records the native Yamato reading 宇米 (ume) alongside the Chinese binomial 梅 (Ch: méi), reflecting his method of bridging Chinese pharmaceutical learning with indigenous Japanese nomenclature. The smoked/charred preparation known as 烏梅 [ubai; Ch: wūméi — 'black plum'], produced by smoking unripe fruit over straw or wood until the flesh blackens and shrivels, is the primary medicinal form referenced; this concentrate intensifies the astringent and antibacterial properties. Fresh fruit, juice, and preserved salt-pickled forms (proto-umeboshi) were also in documented use for digestive complaints.

entities_mentioned.name: 梅 / ume (宇米)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Prunus mume (Siebold & Zucc.) — Japanese apricot, commonly called 'Japanese plum'; family Rosaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Prunus mume is the universally accepted modern identification for the classical 梅 (Ch: méi; Jp: ume). Native to China and naturalized early in Japan. The Honzo Wamyo records the Yamato reading 宇米 (ume), one of the earliest phonetic glosses for this plant in Japanese literature. Distinguished in Japanese botanical history from true plums (Prunus salicina) and from European plums. The fruit is not typically eaten fresh; medicinal and culinary value lies in processed forms: smoked 烏梅 (ubai), salted preserved fruit (umeboshi), and juice extracts.

entities_mentioned.name: 烏梅 / ubai (Ch: wūméi)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Processed fruit of Prunus mume — smoked/charred unripe fruit
entities_mentioned.notes: 烏梅 ('black/crow plum') is the primary pharmaceutical preparation: unripe green ume fruit smoked slowly over straw or rice husks until the skin turns black and the flesh becomes dense and intensely sour. This preparation concentrates malic acid, citric acid, and succinic acid, and was historically credited with antibacterial, antidiarrheal, antiparasitic, and astringent actions. Still officinal in the Japanese Pharmacopoeia (JP) and Traditional Chinese Medicine (TCM) formularies.

results.translator_notes:
1. SOURCE CAVEAT: The source text provided is a modern editorial summary prompt rather than a verbatim passage from the Honzo Wamyo manuscript tradition. The Chinese-language pharmaceutical text reconstructed above draws on the attested content of the Honzo Wamyo (本草和名, 918 CE, compiled by Fukane no Sukehito 深根輔仁) as preserved in later manuscript copies and modern scholarly editions (particularly the Masamune Atsuo 1936 critical edition), supplemented by parallel passages in the Shennong Bencao Jing (神農本草経) and Tang Bencao (唐本草) which Fukane cites for 梅. Readers should be aware this is a reconstructed representative entry, not a direct OCR transcription of a single manuscript page.

2. YAMATO NAME: The phonetic gloss 宇米 (ume) appears in the Honzo Wamyo as Fukane's systematic practice of appending native Japanese readings (和名, yamato-na) to Chinese drug names — a defining and historically significant feature of the text and its contribution to Japanese lexicography.

3. THERAPEUTIC VOCABULARY: Terms like 偏枯不仁 (hemiplegia with loss of sensation) and 死肌 (necrotic/insensate flesh) reflect the classical Sino-Japanese medical framework of qi stagnation and nutritive-qi (ying qi) failure, not modern neurological diagnosis. They are preserved in brackets for historical accuracy.

4. INDIGENOUS JAPANESE OBSERVATION: While ume was introduced from the continent, the Honzo Wamyo is notable for occasionally recording plants and preparations observed independently in Japan. For ume, the text reflects Heian-period court familiarity with the tree as both medicinal and aesthetic (ume was the preeminent poetic flower before sakura displaced it). No claim of unique Japanese indigeneity is made for this species.

5. MODERN PHARMACOLOGY ALIGNMENT: Modern phytochemical research supports the antibacterial (against H. pylori, S. aureus) and digestive-stimulant properties attributed to ume in the Honzo Wamyo, lending the historical record a degree of empirical corroboration.
